description: Figure 1 presents the S-N curves (stress versus number of cycles to failure) obtained from reverse bending fatigue tests on alloy B of the Fe-Mn-Al-C system, specifically comparing longitudinal specimen BL (solid triangles ▲ connected by solid line) and transverse specimen BT (open triangles △ connected by dashed line). The plot displays stress amplitude in MPa on the linear y-axis (ranging from 200 to 500 MPa) against fatigue cycles on a logarithmic x-axis (spanning 10^5 to 2×10^7 cycles). The curves demonstrate that specimen BL exhibits superior fatigue resistance compared to specimen BT, as evidenced by the solid line consistently positioned above the dashed line, indicating higher stress tolerance for equivalent fatigue life. This performance divergence is attributed to the duplex microstructure containing 10% ferrite and 90% austenite, where the ferrite phase acts as a crack arrester when its string-like arrangement is perpendicular to crack propagation in BL specimens, whereas parallel alignment in BT specimens facilitates crack growth across austenite grains. The data supports the conclusion that ferrite phase orientation significantly influences fatigue crack growth resistance in these alloys.

This optical micrograph displays the fatigue crack propagation behavior in specimen BT of a Fe-Mn-Al-C alloy (alloy B tested in the transverse direction) after solution treatment at 1050°C. The micrograph reveals a duplex microstructure comprising approximately 10% ferrite and 90% austenite, with the ferrite phase appearing as light-etched stringers or cellular networks oriented parallel to the crack propagation direction. A prominent fatigue crack is visible traversing the microstructure vertically, propagating directly across the austenite grains without deflection or arrest. The image demonstrates that when the ferrite string arrangement is parallel to the crack propagation direction, as occurs in transverse specimens, the crack can propagate unimpeded through the austenite matrix. This contrasts with longitudinal specimens (BL) where perpendicular ferrite strings impede crack growth and serve as crack arresters. The micrograph includes a 50 μm scale bar for size reference. This visual evidence supports the core conclusion that specimen BT exhibits inferior fatigue resistance compared to specimen BL due to the unfavorable orientation of ferrite stringers relative to the stress axis and crack propagation direction.

This optical micrograph displays the fatigue crack behavior in specimen BL (longitudinal direction) of alloy B (Fe-29Mn-9.1Al-0.6C), which contains approximately 10% ferrite and 90% austenite after solution treatment at 1050°C. The image reveals fatigue cracks that initiated at slip bands within the austenite matrix but were subsequently impeded or arrested by the ferrite phase grains. The ferrite phase appears as bright stringers or bands oriented perpendicular to the crack propagation direction, effectively acting as crack arresters. A scale bar of 50 μm is present. This micrograph directly supports the finding that when the string arrangement of ferrite is perpendicular to the crack propagation direction (as in specimen BL), the soft ferrite grains impede crack growth through crack blunting and work hardening mechanisms, resulting in superior fatigue resistance compared to specimen BT where ferrite strings are parallel to crack propagation.
